---
title: "Task 3: Considerations in High Dimensions"
author: "Aakriti Poudel"
date: "May 18"
format: html
---

## Introduction

This document explores key challenges that arise when fitting regression models to high dimensional data settings where the number of features $p$ exceeds the number of observations $n$, and reflects on the use of generative AI to clarify concepts from Section 6.4 of *An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in Python*.

## Concept to Clarify

The textbook says that adding noise features, variables that have nothing to do with the outcome, makes test error go up, which makes sense. But it also says that adding a feature that does predict the outcome can still make test error go up. I understood the basic idea, but I could not see why a useful variable would hurt the model and how that connects to the bias variance tradeoff.

## Prompt

> I am a graduate student pursuing the Master of Environmental Data Science degree. For my machine learning course, I am reading **Section 6.4, Considerations in High Dimensions**, from the book *An Introduction to Statistical Learning*. I am trying to understand the curse of dimensionality more accurately.
> The textbook states, "Even if they are relevant, the variance incurred in fitting their coefficients may outweigh the reduction in bias that they bring". I understand that noise features affect model performance, but I don't understand why adding a relevant feature can still increase test error in high dimensions.
> Can you explain this in plain, simple language with no jargon and no math? Please use an analogy to make it easier to follow. I also want to understand how this is different from a setting where you have many observations and only a few variables. I am particularly interested in why having too many variables compared to observations makes it hard to get reliable estimates, even when the added variable genuinely helps predict the outcome.

## Generative AI Reflection

I used Claude for this task. My prompt included my background as a graduate student, the exact sentence from the textbook that confused me, and a request for a simple analogy with no math. Claude explained it like this: imagine you are trying to figure out what makes a student do well on an exam, but you only have 5 students. If you look at 3 things about each student, you can spot real patterns. But if you look at 100 things, you start finding patterns that are just coincidence and even a variable that truly matters becomes hard to pin down reliably with so few students. It took three iteration to get a clear answer, and the response matched what the textbook said without introducing anything contradictory.
